text:
On the Dry Bulk side, activity has remained notably strong, more than a handful transactions were concluded this week across a broad range of segments,from Handysize up to Post-Panamaxes. Buyers' appetite remains healthy, underpinning a consistently active dry sale and purchase market.
On the Tanker side, activity has been comparatively more selective, in line with the previous weeks.
At the same time, market participants are closely monitoring geopolitical developments following the signing of the Memorandum of Agreement between the US and Iran. While the agreement has been welcomed as a step towards de-escalation, the longer-term implications remain uncertain, and the market awaits to see greater clarity on the normalization in the Strait of Hormuz during the following weeks.
Dry Cargo
In the Post-Panamax segment, Greek buyers emerged behind the scrubber-fitted CORNELIE OLDENDORFF (ABT 93K DWT, 2011, ZHEJIANG, SCRUBBER), which is understood sold in the high USD 15m range, with delivery in October.
On the Kamsarmax front, Japanese Owners concluded the sale of the MONT BLANC HAWK (ABT 82K DWT, 2017, IMABARI) after inviting offers earlier in the week, achieving a price in the region of USD 32.5m.
The Supramax AFRICAN TERN (ABT 58K DWT, 2013, DALIAN COSCO) has been sold for high USD 19m, whilst the SANTA RITA (ABT 56K DWT, 2010, MITSUI) was acquired by Greek interests for USD 17.2m.
In the Handysize sector, c/o Devbulk have acquired the SHINSUNG ACCORD (ABT 37K DWT, 2015, SAIKI) and the ASTRO PROPUS (ABT 38K DWT, 2014, IMABARI) at USD 19m each. Separately, the DEVBULK IMABARI (ABT 29K DWT, 2009, IMABARI, OHBS/GC) has been sold to Turkish buyers for USD 10.5m.
Tanker
On the Aframax segment, the SAMOS (ABT 105K DWT, 2010, SUMITOMO) achieved a price around region USD mid-40m

Tanker
On the Aframax segment, the SAMOS (ABT 105K DWT, 2010, SUMITOMO) achieved a price around region USD mid-40m's basis Q3 delivery.
Elsewhere, the MR SANDPIPER PACIFIC (ABT 51K DWT, 2013, SUNGDONG) was sold for USD 32m.
(All details given in good faith and for information only, without guarantee/prejudice)

Capesize Rates Remain Seasonally Strong Despite Recent Drop - The dry bulk market continues to demonstrate
Source: Breakwave Advisors
snippet: Capesize Rates Remain Seasonally Strong Despite Recent Drop - The dry bulk market continues to demonstrate Source: Breakwave Advisors

text:
The US-Iran ceasefire has not yet changed much on the water, while the impact of the signing of the Memorandum of Agreement betweenthe US and Iran on the Hormuz chokepoint remains to be seen. For now, uncertainty surrounding transit through the Strait persists, with the number of monitored transits still well below pre-conflict levels.
In dry bulk, rates softened with the recording a week-on-week decline of approximately 7.3%. On tankers, in contrast to the rate easing seen in previous weeks, a slight increase was observed this week, particularly across the larger tanker segments.
Overall, we observed healthy S&P activity this week in both the Dry and Wet markets, despite fluctuations in freight rates. On Dry, while demand for larger tonnage (Kamsarmaxes through to Capes) remains, activity this week mainly concentrated on geared tonnage ranging from Handysize up to Ultramax vessels. On the tanker side, while enquiry for tonnage has been maintained, buyers are adopting a more cautious approach in light of the ongoing geopolitical developments
Dry Cargo
The Kamsarmax SEACON HAMBURG (ABT 86K DWT, 2023, HUANGPU WENCHONG) has been committed at USD 37.5m with a time charter attached until February-June 2028. For comparison, the same aged sister vessels SEACON VANCOUVER and SEACON OSLO were sold two months ago for USD 36.35m each basis a charter free delivery between October - November 2026.
On the Ultramax front, the ULTRA SAKA (ex-name MITSOS - ABT 64K DWT, 2013, CHENGXI), was acquired by Chinese interests for USD 23.25m. Interestingly, current sellers bought the MITSOS, only a few months back at region USD 20.5m.
On the Supramax size, the Japanese-built OCEAN BRIGHT (ABT 56K DWT, 2013, MITSUI) was sold for xs USD 19m, whereas the LILA CUMBERLAND (ABT 56K DWT, 2013, JIANGSU HANTONG) was sold in the high USD 16's m levels to c/o ADNOC L&S.
The Handysize DARYA KRISHNA (ABT 34K DWT,

alongside expanded export programs from ADNOC, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and Iran.

snippet: Source asset: reports/hellenic/shipbuilding/pdfs/2026-06-30_breakwave-tanker-report-6-30-2026_breakwavetankersjune302026report_19efd5a76b05.pdf  [Page 1] Tanker Shipping Bi-Weekly Industry Report June

text:
30D: -26.6% ↓
rapid shift in oil market sentiment from supply anxiety to an oversupply narrative has
YTD: 318.8% ↑
driven crude prices back to pre-war levels, despite little change in structural reality.
While global inventories continue a rapid drawdown and uncertainty persists over YOY: 412.1% ↑
the pace of Arabian Gulf (AG) export recovery, a steep decline in Chinese spot
market purchasing has put severe downward pressure on global pricing. The
primary driver of this deflation is not necessarily the volume of barrels transiting the Reuters: BWET.K
Hormuz straits (which is admittedly high in such a short period of time), but rather Bloomberg: BWET US
Exchange: NYSE
the notable lack of appetite from Chinese refiners to accumulate stockpiles at pre-
conflict rates. Whether this pullback represents a structural shift in Chinese demand
(economy) or a temporary buying pause remains to be seen; however, the
Breakwave Advisors Research
immediate seaborne market is left fundamentally imbalanced with a surplus of research@breakwaveadvisors.com
prompt spot oil supply.
• Our Long-term View - The tanker market has been recovering from a long period
of staggered rates as the growth in new vessel supply shrunk while oil demand
remained elevated in line with the global economy. The recent rapid increase in
freight rates has led to significant new vessel ordering, with the orderbook now
standing at well above average levels, and although in the near term such a
supply/demand misbalance is small, we expect a meaningful negative balance to
develop longer term leading to a industry downcycle.

text:
S&P activity remained steady despite the relatively more cautious approach emerging amid the ongoing geopoliticaldevelopments in the Middle East.
Market participants continue to monitor the evolving situation around the Strait of Hormuz and the US-Iran peace talks.
Although vessel transits through the strait have gradually increased, uncertainty over the security of safe passage continues to weigh on investment decisions, prompting many buyers to remain on the sidelines until greater clarity emerges
Dry freight rates continue to remain healthy with this week's BDI closing at significantly higher levels than last week's, which is also justified by high volumes of enquiries that we continue to receive especially on the Handysize and Supramax space.
Tanker rates have eased from the recent highs driven by regional tensions but continue to hold at levels that owners consider satisfactory, supporting asset values.
Dry Cargo
On the Kamsarmax space, the modern SCION MATHILDA (82,144 DWT, 2024, JIANGSU NEW HANTONG) has been concluded to C/O Castor at levels of USD 41.9m.
Also, the scrubber fitted AC YOUTH (82,623 DWT, 2007, TSUNEISHI ZOSEN, SCRUBBER) was sold at USD 15m.
On the Panamax sector, the vintage unit MARINICKI C (76,629 DWT, 2005, IMABARI SB MARUGAME) was sold to undisclosed interests, achieving a price of USD 11.7m.
On geared tonnage, the Supramax LAGONDA (55,773 DWT, 2011, I.H.I YOHOHAMA, C:4X35T) was sold at USD 18.25m basis SS DD due.
Moreover, two Japanese built Handysizes invited bids this week, with the eco unit ATLANTIC STAR (37,065 DWT, 2018, OSHIMA SHIPBUILDING, C:4X30T) now sold in the region of USD 26m, while the ASAHI OCEAN (32,085 DWT, 2013, HAKODATE DOCK, C:4X30.5T) achieved levels in the region of USD 15.2m.
According to unconfirmed reports, a 2020 Japanese-built Ultramax has also changed hands at a price in the region of USD 36.5m.
Tanker

text:
As many had anticipated, the U.S.-Iran ceasefire has failed to hold, with renewed hostilities threatening to underminethe fragile optimism that the traffic in the Straits of Hormuz will be fully restored sooner than later.
The latest developments have reinforced concerns that geopolitical risks remain elevated and continue to influence the decisions of Buyers and Sellers in all segments.
On the commodity side, coal demand in the Far East is expected to strengthen, with Vietnam projected to reach record coal imports in 2026.
This growth is driven by government plans to expand the country's power generation capacity through alternative energy sources, including coal.
More broadly, this reflects a trend that other energy-import-dependent countries may also follow if tensions in the Middle East persist and continue to disrupt global energy supply routes, suggesting an optimistic landscape for seaborne trade in the short-and-medium-term.
These developments have been reflected in this week's Drybulk market, where Capesize experienced a strong week, with the Baltic Capesize Index closing today at 4,655, representing a 13.5% week-on-week increase.
In Tankers, VLCC activity in the Middle East has strengthened as well, further bolstered by Saudi Aramco's aggressive price cuts for its August crude export program and the UAE's record-high crude oil production in June following its exit from the OPEC.
Fixture volumes have increased as a result, although it remains uncertain whether fixtures from inside the Strait of Hormuz will conclude.
Dry Cargo
Activity in the Ultramax market saw the HAATO (ABT 61,472 DWT, 2011, SHIN KASADO DOCK, C:4X30.5T) committed to C/O ADNOC at USD 23.5m, with the deal concluded basis freshly passed surveys.
Elsewhere, in the Supramax sector EVEREST (ABT 57,480 DWT, 2012, STX SB (JINHAE), C:4X30T) reportedly sold for a price in the low USD 16m's.
Additionally, the modern Handysize JNS PHOENIX (ABT 40,552 DWT, 2025 JIANGMEN NANYANG, C:4X30T) has been committed at a price of excess USD 34m after calling for offers.
Further down the size spectrum the HTK LUCKY (ABT 28,481 DWT, 2003

Further down the size spectrum the HTK LUCKY (ABT 28,481 DWT, 2003 IMABARI SB (IMABARI), C:4X30.5T) changed hands, with the transaction agreed at around USD 6m.
Tanker
Although we understand several negotiations to be ongoing, there are no new tanker sales to report this week.

and continue to influence the decisions of Buyers and Sellers in all segments.
On the commodity side, coal demand in the Far East is expected to strengthen, with Vietnam projected
to reach record coal imports in 2026. This growth is driven by government plans to expand the country's
power generation capacity through alternative energy sources, including coal.
More broadly, this reflects a trend that other energy-import-dependent countries may also follow if
tensions in the Middle East persist and continue to disrupt global energy supply routes, suggesting an
optimistic landscape for seaborne trade in the short-and-medium-term.
These developments have been reflected in this week's Drybulk market, where Capesize experienced
a strong week, with the Baltic Capesize Index closing today at 4,655, representing a 13.5% week-on-
week increase.
In Tankers, VLCC activity in the Middle East has strengthened as well, further bolstered by Saudi
Aramco's aggressive price cuts for its August crude export program and the UAE's record-high crude
oil production in June following its exit from the OPEC. Fixture volumes have increased as a result,
although it remains uncertain whether fixtures from inside the Strait of Hormuz will conclude.

Bloomberg: BDRY US

Exchange: NYSE
electric vehicle adoption and alternative energy integration continue to erode the
country's baseline transportation fuel requirements. Consequently, despite a recent Breakwave Advisors Research
research@breakwaveadvisors.com
peace breakthrough easing global supply constraints, China's underlying domestic
consumption remains structurally impaired, leaving its broader economic growth
increasingly dependent on the export market to sustain current production targets.
• Our Long-term View - The last few years have been characterized by increased
geopolitical uncertainty. Going forward, we expect such events to continue to affect
global trade and have a meaningful impact on effective vessel supply. Combined
with the potential for a multi-year cyclical rebound in China's economic activity
following the recent economic turmoil, dry bulk shipping should experience higher
volatility on top of a secular tightness driven by stable bulk commodity demand and
rather steady fleet growth.

text:
Nearing the end of the second week after the interim peace deal between Iran and United States began to break down, and amid renewed attackson vessels in the MEG, we've now reached the lowest daily total tonnage transit number through the SoH at 0.2m GT on Monday, amounting to >95% below typical daily volumes. Following the temporary easing of sanctions, Iran increased crude exports during the brief truce, leading to a rise in floating oil supplies. However, crude sales have remained subdued as China's independent refiners have opted for cheaper cargo from Iraq, the UAE, and Qatar.
At the same time, energy shipping markets remain at very elevated levels across all segments as reflected on S&P asset prices.
This week saw healthy ordering activity spread across multiple shipping segments, including Suezmax tankers, MR tankers, LNG carriers, and VLGCs. Meanwhile, benchmark newbuild prices in the tanker market have continued climbing on the back of robust demand for new vessels - VLCCs NB prices are now 2% higher since the start of the year, Suezmaxes are up 4%, and MRs have risen by 5%.
In the secondhand market, the dry bulk sector once again accounted for the majority of transaction activity this week, with prominent demand observed for geared tonnage ranging from larger Handysize up to Ultramax bulk carriers. For comparison purposes in ECO Ultramaxes, although not identically designed, BLUE AKIHABARA (ABT 61,630 DWT, 2014, NANTONG COSCO KHI (NACKS), CR:4X30T) achieved a price of excess USD 25m this week, when in May the two-year-younger sister-vessels HUAYANG ROSE and HUAYANG LILY (ABT 63,562 DWT, 2016, CHINA SHIPPING INDUSTRY (JIANGSU), CR:4X30) were understood to have been concluded at xs USD 25m each basis an enbloc sale.
Dry Cargo
In the Capesize sector this week, the HEROIC (ABT 182,060 DWT, 2010, ODENSE LINDO) was concluded at USD 32.8m, to European interests.
Meanwhile, the eco Ultramax BLUE AKIHABARA (ABT 61,630 DWT, 2014, N

:4X30) were understood to have been concluded at xs USD 25m each basis an enbloc sale.
Dry Cargo
In the Capesize sector this week, the HEROIC (ABT 182,060 DWT, 2010, ODENSE LINDO) was concluded at USD 32.8m, to European interests.
Meanwhile, the eco Ultramax BLUE AKIHABARA (ABT 61,630 DWT, 2014, NANTONG COSCO KHI (NACKS), CR:4X30T, Electronic M/E) after receiving offers in early July, has now been concluded at excess USD 25m, with Greek buyers reported behind the acquisition.
Elsewhere, in the Supramax sector, the IVS CRIMSON CREEK (ABT 57,945 DWT, 2014, SK TOYOHASHI SB, CR:4X30.5T, Electronic M/E) also invited offers this week and we understand has seen excess USD 23.5 million.
Tanker
Although we understand several negotiations to be ongoing, there are no new tanker sales to report this week.
Source: Clarkson Platou (Hellas) ltd
